findings.title: Default continual mode changes existing run_loop behavior
findings.description: Existing callers of `unciv_train.run_loop` now get continual training by default, including blind/rich/structured runs that previously trained fresh each round. Worse, resuming an existing pre-v5 run with `--resume` can generate the next shard first and then fail when `_load_warm` discovers there is no `opt_round_*.pt` sidecar.
findings.rationale: This is a classic default-change regression: old experiment scripts can silently produce non-comparable results or waste a generation round before failing. The new behavior should be explicit, not surprise every existing caller.
findings.suggested_addition: Make `--continual` opt-in, or update all existing shipped drivers to pass `--no-continual` where old semantics are intended. Also validate required ckpt/opt sidecars before starting generation for `start_round > 0`.
source_excerpts: ap.add_argument("--continual", action=argparse.BooleanOptionalAction, default=True,
if args.continual and warm_net is None and r > 0:   # --resume restart: load warm net+opt from disk
A pre-v5 run has no opt sidecar — run with --no-continual or start fresh from round 0.

findings.title: Trainer return shape breaks existing 2-tuple callers
findings.description: The actor-critic trainer functions now return `(net, stats, optimizer)` instead of `(net, stats)`. Any existing script/notebook/test doing `net, stats = train_actor_critic_structured(...)` now raises `ValueError: too many values to unpack`.
findings.rationale: This is avoidable API breakage for plumbing that only `run_loop` needs. For a research codebase, notebooks and one-off scripts are real callers, and this will bite them immediately.
findings.suggested_addition: Add a minimal compatibility shim, e.g. a `return_optimizer=False` keyword defaulting to old 2-tuple behavior, and have `run_loop` pass `return_optimizer=True`. At minimum, grep/update all in-repo callers and document the breaking change.
source_excerpts: net, stats, _opt = train_actor_critic_structured(   # v5: trainer now returns (net, stats, optimizer)
) -> tuple[object, dict, object]:
return net, stats, optimizer

findings.title: run_v5.sh masks failed arms and exits successfully
findings.description: `run_v5.sh` logs failures but keeps going and can finish with `DONE` and exit code 0 even if an arm failed, analysis failed, or the bench produced no verdict. That makes automation and future humans trust incomplete `acceptance_v5.json` output.
findings.rationale: This is too clever for a one-off driver: swallowing failures saves nothing and makes debugging worse after a multi-hour run. If the experiment failed, the script should say so in its exit status.
findings.suggested_addition: Track a `FAILED=0` flag, set it on any arm/analyze/bench failure, and `exit 1` at the end if non-zero. If continuing to collect partial artifacts is desired, still make the final status non-zero.
source_excerpts: "$PY" -m unciv_train.run_loop "${COMMON[@]}" --rung small --micro-batch-steps 0 \
--out "$ROOT/structured-small-Medium" || log "ARM A FAILED (continuing)"
|| log "ANALYZE $ARM FAILED"
2>/dev/null | grep -E 'BENCH\||verdict=' || log "BENCH produced no verdict line"
log "DONE — per-arm results in $ROOT/*/acceptance_v5.json"

findings.title: Negative micro-batch size crashes with an unhelpful error
findings.description: `--micro-batch-steps -1` is accepted by argparse and enters the micro-batch path because it is truthy and `< n`. The snapshot loop produces no chunks and `torch.cat([])` crashes with a low-level error instead of telling the caller the argument is invalid.
findings.rationale: A single guard beats making future developers debug a PyTorch stack trace from a typo. This is exactly the kind of edge-case validation that should live at the boundary, not become tribal knowledge.
findings.suggested_addition: Validate `args.micro_batch_steps >= 0` in `run_loop.main`, or normalize `micro_batch_steps <= 0` to `None` before `_optimize_actor_critic`.
source_excerpts: ap.add_argument("--micro-batch-steps", type=int, default=0,
mb = getattr(args, "micro_batch_steps", 0) or None
use_micro = bool(micro_batch_steps) and forward_chunk_fn is not None and micro_batch_steps < n
for lo in range(0, n, micro_batch_steps):

findings.title: Resume only warm-loads at the FIRST round after restart, not later
findings.description: The gate `if args.continual and warm_net is None and r > 0:` only fires on the first loop iteration after a restart. But `start_round` is derived from curve.csv row count, and the loop runs `range(start_round, args.rounds)`. If a run is interrupted such that curve.csv has N rows but the ckpt/opt sidecars for round N-1 are the ones present, the load is correct — BUT note `warm_net` is set to None at function entry, so on a fresh (non-resume) run starting at round 0, the gate never fires (correct), and on resume the disk-load happens exactly once. The subtle break: the curve-row count (`start_round`) and the latest surviving ckpt round can disagree after pruning keeps only the last 3 — if `start_round-1 > ` the newest pruned-away ckpt this is fine, but if a crash left curve.csv with row r written (the 'commit marker') yet the round-r ckpt/opt never flushed, `_load_warm` loads round `start_round-1 == r-1`'s sidecars while the gen ONNX path may expect round r. There is no assertion that `start_round-1` matches the highest available ckpt.
findings.rationale: Architect lens: the resume contract now spans three artifacts (curve.csv, ckpt, opt) written at different points with no transactional commit ordering (FND-0007 flagged this at design; the diff did NOT implement 'append curve row LAST as commit marker'). A torn write between ckpt save and curve-row append silently resumes from a stale optimizer.
findings.suggested_addition: Add an assertion in `_load_warm` that `start-1` equals the max round N for which both `ckpt_round_N.pt` and `opt_round_N.pt` exist; if curve.csv claims more rows than the newest surviving sidecar, fail fast with the mismatch. One log line: `[resume] curve says round={start} but newest ckpt=round={N}`.
source_excerpts: if args.continual and warm_net is None and r > 0:   # --resume restart: load warm net+opt from disk
for stale in (out / f"ckpt_round_{r - 3}.pt", out / f"opt_round_{r - 3}.pt"):

findings.title: Curve-row commit ordering not implemented — torn write poisons resume
findings.target_section: main loop:286-326 (ckpt save then later curve.csv write)
findings.description: The design (FND-0007) specified writing ckpt+opt then appending the curve.csv row LAST as the atomic commit marker. The diff makes ckpt/opt writes atomic individually via os.replace, but the curve.csv row is still appended elsewhere in the loop with no ordering guarantee relative to the sidecar writes. A crash between the curve-row append and the sidecar replace (or vice versa) leaves `start_round` (from curve rows) pointing at a round whose sidecars are absent or stale, defeating the fail-fast.
findings.rationale: Architect: the multi-artifact resume invariant is only as strong as its weakest write-ordering. Individually-atomic files do not compose into an atomic logical commit; the consistency boundary is unprotected.
findings.suggested_addition: Document and enforce write order: `_atomic_torch_save(ckpt)` → `_atomic_torch_save(opt)` → only then append the curve.csv row. Add a comment marking the curve-row append as the commit marker, and a test that asserts resume from a state with a curve row but missing opt sidecar raises FileNotFoundError (the path exists in `_load_warm` but is untested for this torn-write case).
source_excerpts: _atomic_torch_save(net.state_dict(), out / f"ckpt_round_{r}.pt")

if not ckpt.is_file() or not optf.is_file():
        raise FileNotFoundError(

findings.title: Pruning keeps last-3 sidecars but resume may need round r-1 after a multi-round gap
findings.description: Prune deletes `ckpt_round_{r-3}.pt` / `opt_round_{r-3}.pt` each round, keeping a 3-round window. Resume needs round `start_round-1`. These are consistent ONLY because the loop always advances by 1 and resume always restarts at the next round. But the prune is gated inside the same `if` block as shard cleanup (`r % args.keep_shards`-style); if `--keep-shards` differs from the implicit 3-window, or a run is resumed and immediately interrupted before completing round `start_round`, the round `start_round-1` sidecar could already have been pruned by the PRIOR process at its round `start_round-1+3`. The two retention windows (keep_shards for round dirs, hardcoded 3 for sidecars) are independent and unsynchronized.
findings.rationale: Architect: two retention policies with different windows governing artifacts that must stay mutually consistent for resume is a latent coupling bug — the hardcoded `3` is a magic number not tied to the resume requirement (which only needs 1 prior round).
findings.suggested_addition: Tie the sidecar keep-window to an explicit constant (e.g. `RESUME_KEEP = 2`) with a comment stating 'resume needs round r-1; keep 2 for safety against torn prune'. Add a test that after pruning, `ckpt_round_{r-1}.pt` and `opt_round_{r-1}.pt` always exist.
source_excerpts: # v5: prune stale ckpt/opt sidecars, keeping the last 3 (resume needs round r-1) — FND-0010
for stale in (out / f"ckpt_round_{r - 3}.pt", out / f"opt_round_{r - 3}.pt"):

findings.title: Diverged warm round silently carries forward unchanged weights, masking plateau
findings.description: On divergence both paths restore safe_state/safe_opt and return early with `diverged=True`. Under continual carry (run_loop sets `warm_net, warm_opt = net, opt`), a diverged round propagates the PRIOR round's weights as the next round's warm start. FND-0028 flagged exactly this: the winrate curve plateaus indistinguishably from genuine convergence, corrupting the AC1/AC2 conclusion. The diff records `diverged` in stats/metrics but the run continues training/evaluating on the carried-forward (unlearning) net with no abort or loud flag at the loop level.
findings.rationale: Architect/domain: the experiment's entire deliverable is 'does warm-start clear 28.9%'. A silent diverged-then-plateau run answers that question with a false negative and burns hours of compute (the decisions.md explicitly chose 'no auto-abort').
findings.suggested_addition: Not a redesign — add a loop-level log + metrics counter when `stats.get('diverged')` is True, and have analyze_v5 surface any round with diverged=1 in acceptance_v5.json (flagging the ceiling eval as suspect). One assertion in RESULTS path: refuse to claim a pass if any round diverged.
source_excerpts:
net.load_state_dict(safe_state)
                opt.load_state_dict(safe_opt)               # v5: restore optimizer moments too
                stats["diverged"] = True
                return net, stats

if args.continual:
            warm_net, warm_opt = net, opt

findings.title: Micro-batch mean_value uses sum/n but mean_adv/grad_norm computed on full batch — stat inconsistency
findings.description: In the micro path, `mean_value = mv_sum / n` accumulates `val.detach().sum()` per chunk (correct), but `mean_adv = float(adv.mean().item())` reads the full-batch adv tensor while loss/policy/value/entropy are size-weighted chunk sums. This is fine numerically, but `grad_norm` is measured on the accumulated gradient AFTER all chunk backwards — which equals the whole-batch grad norm only if chunk weighting is exact. The test asserts grad_norm within 1e-4, but there is no assertion that the per-stat reconstruction (pl_sum, vl_sum, ent_sum) matches the whole-batch components individually, only the combined loss. A weighting bug in one term could cancel in the total loss yet diverge the actual gradient.
findings.rationale: Architect: the equivalence test guards the aggregate loss and final weights, but the per-component stats (logged to metrics.jsonl for debugging) could silently misreport, making production debugging of a real divergence harder.
findings.suggested_addition: Add to test_microbatch: assert `abs(whole_stats['value_loss'] - micro_stats['value_loss']) < 1e-5` and the same for `policy_loss` and `entropy` individually, not just the combined `loss`.
source_excerpts:
stats.update(loss=loss_total, policy_loss=pl_sum, value_loss=vl_sum, entropy=ent_sum,
                         mean_adv=float(adv.mean().item()), mean_value=mv_sum / n, grad_norm=float(gnorm))
assert abs(whole_stats["loss"] - micro_stats["loss"]) < 1e-5

findings.title: Bash driver script swallows arm failures and exits 0
findings.description: The script catches failures using `|| log '... FAILED'` but never sets a failure flag or exits with a non-zero status. If all training arms crash, the script will still exit 0.
findings.rationale: CI/CD pipelines and cron schedulers rely on exit codes to trigger Sev-2 alerts. A silent failure means the pipeline is broken but on-call is never paged, leading to stale models in production.
findings.suggested_addition: Initialize `ERR=0`, use `|| { log '...'; ERR=1; }` for each step, and add `exit $ERR` at the end of the script.
source_excerpts: || log "ARM A FAILED (continuing)"
|| log "ANALYZE $ARM FAILED"

findings.title: Atomic save leaks .tmp files on crash or disk-full
findings.description: If `torch.save` is interrupted by SIGKILL, OOM, or `NoSpaceLeftOnDevice`, the `.tmp` file is orphaned. Because the filename includes the round number, these temporary files will accumulate across retries.
findings.rationale: Unbounded disk growth from orphaned temporary files causes cascading node failures. If a node runs out of disk, leaving the partial file behind guarantees the next run will also fail.
findings.suggested_addition: Wrap `torch.save` in a `try...except Exception:` block that unlinks the `.tmp` file (`tmp.unlink(missing_ok=True)`) before re-raising the exception.
source_excerpts: torch.save(obj, tmp)
os.replace(tmp, path)

findings.title: Checkpoint pruning crashes the multi-hour training loop on file locks
findings.description: `Path.unlink()` raises `OSError` or `PermissionError` if the file is locked by another process (e.g., a backup daemon, antivirus, or NFS glitch). This will crash the entire training loop.
findings.rationale: Non-critical background cleanup should never be in the critical path of a stateful, long-running job. A transient file lock shouldn't waste 8 hours of compute.
findings.suggested_addition: Wrap the `stale.unlink(missing_ok=True)` call in a `try...except OSError: pass` block.
source_excerpts: for stale in (out / f"ckpt_round_{r - 3}.pt", out / f"opt_round_{r - 3}.pt"):
stale.unlink(missing_ok=True)

findings.title: Missing explicit garbage collection between continual rounds
findings.description: The loop carries `warm_net` and `warm_opt` in-memory while repeatedly allocating massive trajectory batches. Python's cyclic GC may not run promptly enough to clear the previous round's batch tensors before the next round allocates memory.
findings.rationale: Long-running ML pipelines are highly susceptible to memory fragmentation and OOMs. Relying on implicit GC in a tight loop with large allocations often pages on-call with OOMKilled errors at 3am.
findings.suggested_addition: Add `import gc` and call `gc.collect()` at the end of the round loop (after `v = verdict(rows)` or shard cleanup) to force memory reclamation.
source_excerpts: warm_net, warm_opt = net, opt                   # carry the persistent pair to next round

findings.title: Analysis script exits 0 when required ONNX artifacts are missing
findings.description: If the upstream training job completely fails to produce models, `_last_onnx` returns `None`. The script logs a note to the JSON and exits 0, masking the missing artifact.
findings.rationale: Downstream deployment gates or monitoring dashboards will parse the 0 exit code and the valid JSON as a successful run, hiding the upstream pipeline failure.
findings.suggested_addition: If `onnx is None` and `--skip-ceiling-eval` was NOT explicitly requested, log an error to stderr and call `sys.exit(1)`.
source_excerpts: if args.skip_ceiling_eval or onnx is None:
result["note"] = "ceiling eval skipped or no onnx; winrates are the 80-game per-round evals"

findings.title: run_v5.sh can report DONE after failed arms
findings.description: Both training arms, both analyses, and the bench step swallow failures with `|| log ...` and the script still reaches `DONE`. A failed ARM A can produce no final model, skip the bench entirely, and still exit successfully to the orchestrator.
findings.rationale: QA needs the experiment driver to have a verifiable pass/fail signal. Right now production failures become log-only events, so CI/background supervision can mark an invalid experiment as shipped.
findings.suggested_addition: Track a `FAILED=1` flag for any arm/analyze/bench failure, assert required artifacts exist for each arm, and `exit $FAILED` at the end. For the bench gate, fail if no verdict line or if the verdict is below threshold.
source_excerpts:
"$PY" -m unciv_train.run_loop "${COMMON[@]}" --rung small --micro-batch-steps 0 \
    --out "$ROOT/structured-small-Medium" || log "ARM A FAILED (continuing)"

"$PY" -m unciv_train.run_loop "${COMMON[@]}" --rung medium --micro-batch-steps "$MB" \
    --out "$ROOT/structured-medium-Medium" || log "ARM B FAILED (continuing)"
|| log "ANALYZE $ARM FAILED"

fi
log "DONE — per-arm results in $ROOT/*/acceptance_v5.json"

findings.title: AC6 ONNX parity test never compares logits
findings.description: The test docstring claims torch-vs-ONNX logit parity, but the implementation stops after asserting the exported output names. A regression where the exported ONNX is wired to the wrong warm net, stale weights, or wrong tensor inputs would still pass.
findings.rationale: This is the acceptance test for the on-policy carryover claim. Without an actual fixed-sample inference comparison, QA cannot verify the shipped artifact matches the warm net that generated the round.
findings.suggested_addition: Add a deterministic structured sample, run `net(...)` under torch and `sess.run(...)` under onnxruntime, and assert tech/policy logits match within the established parity tolerance.
source_excerpts:
"""AC6: the warm net a round starts from produces logits matching the ONNX exported from it — the
    on-policy guarantee (warm net == gen net). Skips cleanly if onnxruntime is unavailable."""
# A torch forward of the SAME (warm) net vs the exported ONNX must agree on tech+policy logits.
sess = ort.InferenceSession(str(onnx_path))

assert out_names == [C.OUTPUT_TECH, C.OUTPUT_POLICY], \
        f"export must drop value head, expose exactly tech+policy; got {out_names}"

findings.title: run_loop default silently changes existing experiments
findings.description: `--continual` defaults to true, so existing callers of `unciv_train.run_loop` that do not pass any new flag now get warm-started net/optimizer behavior instead of the previous fresh-per-round behavior. Existing baseline scripts or ad hoc reruns can silently produce non-comparable curves.
findings.rationale: This is a backward-compat regression risk: behavior that worked before this diff changes without the caller opting in. QA cannot tell whether a future baseline result is v4-style or v5-style unless every caller is audited.
findings.suggested_addition: Either make continual mode opt-in and pass `--continual` only from `run_v5.sh`, or add an explicit compatibility test/smoke script proving existing acceptance drivers pass `--no-continual` and log the mode prominently at startup.
source_excerpts:
ap.add_argument("--continual", action=argparse.BooleanOptionalAction, default=True,
                    help="v5: carry (net, optimizer) across rounds (warm-start). --no-continual ⇒ "

net=(warm_net if args.continual else None),
            optimizer=(warm_opt if args.continual else None))

if args.continual:
            warm_net, warm_opt = net, opt

findings.title: Resume does not validate checkpoint configuration
findings.description: Resume only checks that ckpt and optimizer files exist, then relies on `load_state_dict` shape compatibility. Same-shaped but semantically different runs, changed learning rate, or changed schema/ruleset can resume silently; optimizer `load_state_dict` will also overwrite the newly constructed `args.lr` without telling the caller.
findings.rationale: A resumed run that uses the wrong optimizer/config still looks successful but invalidates the experiment. QA needs a concrete invariant for stored state compatibility, not just absence of shape errors.
findings.suggested_addition: Save a small metadata sidecar per checkpoint with variant, rung, dims, schema/ruleset fingerprint, lr, and round; on `_load_warm`, assert it matches current args before generation/training and log the loaded optimizer param group lr.
source_excerpts:
if not ckpt.is_file() or not optf.is_file():
        raise FileNotFoundError(
net.load_state_dict(torch.load(ckpt, weights_only=True))   # raises on shape/rung mismatch

opt = torch.optim.Adam(net.parameters(), lr=args.lr)
    opt.load_state_dict(torch.load(optf, weights_only=True))
print(f"[resume] warm-loaded net+opt from {ckpt.name} + {optf.name}")

findings.title: Negative micro-batch size crashes with obscure torch error
findings.description: `--micro-batch-steps` is parsed as any integer and `_optimize_actor_critic` treats any truthy value less than `n` as enabled. A value like `-1` enters the micro-batch path, produces no chunks, and fails later at `torch.cat` instead of giving an actionable argument error.
findings.rationale: This is an edge-case input validation gap. A typo in the long-running experiment driver should fail immediately with a clear message before any generation/training work is spent.
findings.suggested_addition: Validate `micro_batch_steps >= 0` in argparse or normalize in `_optimize_actor_critic`; add a small test that `--micro-batch-steps -1` exits with a clear error.
source_excerpts: ap.add_argument("--micro-batch-steps", type=int, default=0,
mb = getattr(args, "micro_batch_steps", 0) or None
use_micro = bool(micro_batch_steps) and forward_chunk_fn is not None and micro_batch_steps < n

for lo in range(0, n, micro_batch_steps):
                hi = min(lo + micro_batch_steps, n)

findings.title: bench-onnx invocation reuses gradle_selfplay with Medium map but no map-size validation
findings.description: The bench-onnx gate calls `run_loop.gradle_selfplay(['bench-onnx','$FINAL_ABS','200','Medium','$TH','777000'],3600.0)` with a hardcoded 'Medium' map argument. If the ONNX model was trained on a different map size (e.g. Tiny) or if the bench-onnx subprocess silently accepts a different map than the model expects, the throughput ratio will be measured against the wrong workload — producing a misleading gate verdict that could pass a model that's too slow on the actual Medium map.
findings.rationale: A throughput gate that measures the wrong thing is a silent acceptance — the model passes the gate but underperforms in the real eval workload. This is a classic red-team finding: the gate's input is not validated against the model's training context, so a configuration drift (e.g. someone changes the arm's map size but forgets to update the bench-onnx line) goes undetected.
findings.suggested_addition: Add a pre-bench assertion: extract the map size from the arm's metrics.jsonl or from the run_v5.sh environment and validate that the bench-onnx map argument matches. At minimum, log the map size used for bench-onnx alongside the verdict line so a human can spot a mismatch in the output.
source_excerpts: FINAL_ABS="$(cd "$(dirname "$FINAL")" && pwd)/$(basename "$FINAL")"
"$PY" -c "from unciv_train import run_loop as R; print(R.gradle_selfplay(['bench-onnx','$FINAL_ABS','200','Medium','$TH','777000'],3600.0))"

findings.title: analyze_v5 ceiling eval uses fixed seed 4242424 but the arm's gen model may have been trained with a different eval seed
findings.description: The ceiling eval hardcodes `--eval-seed 4242424` for the 200-game z-test. If the arm was trained with a different eval seed (e.g. the run_loop default `--eval-seed 999000`), the ceiling eval's opponent behavior is drawn from a different random sequence than the per-round 80-game evals. This is intentional (apples-to-apples vs v4), but there is no cross-check that the arm's curve.csv was produced with a compatible seed — a researcher re-running with a custom seed could get a ceiling result that doesn't match the per-round curve, causing confusion.
findings.rationale: In a multi-hour autonomous experiment, a seed mismatch between per-round evals and the ceiling eval produces a curve that doesn't connect to the final number — the researcher sees a winrate jump/drop at the last data point and can't tell if it's the extra games or the seed change. This is a data-integrity footgun, not a correctness bug.
findings.suggested_addition: Log the eval seed used for per-round evals (read from the arm's metrics.jsonl or a new metadata file) and print a warning if it differs from the ceiling eval seed. Include both seeds in acceptance_v5.json so the researcher can spot the discrepancy.
source_excerpts: ap.add_argument("--eval-seed", type=int, default=4242424)
ev = run_loop.evaluate(onnx, args.ceiling_games, args.turn_cap, args.threads, args.eval_seed, args.gradle_timeout, "Medium")

findings.title: run_v5.sh ARM B failure is logged but script continues — ceiling eval runs on potentially broken arm
findings.target_section: ARM B invocation + ceiling eval loop (lines 42-49)
findings.description: ARM B runs with `|| log "ARM B FAILED (continuing)"` — if the medium-rung arm OOMs or diverges, the script logs the failure and proceeds to the ceiling eval loop. The ceiling eval loop iterates over both arm directories unconditionally. If ARM B produced no ONNX files (or a partial/corrupt one), `analyze_v5` will either fail silently (skip-ceiling-eval path) or attempt to evaluate a broken model — wasting compute and producing a misleading acceptance_v5.json.
findings.rationale: A failed training arm that still gets a ceiling eval is a data-integrity risk: the results file will contain a ceiling entry for a model that never completed training, and a human reading RESULTS.md later may not notice the 'FAILED' log line buried in stdout. The experiment should refuse to evaluate an arm that didn't complete.
findings.suggested_addition: After each arm, set a flag (e.g. `ARM_A_OK=1`). In the ceiling eval loop, skip arms whose flag is not set, or at minimum check that `policy_round_15.onnx` exists before invoking analyze_v5. Log a clear 'SKIPPING ceiling eval for $ARM (arm failed)' message.
source_excerpts: "$PY" -m unciv_train.run_loop "${COMMON[@]}" --rung medium --micro-batch-steps "$MB" --out "$ROOT/structured-medium-Medium" || log "ARM B FAILED (continuing)"

for ARM in structured-small-Medium structured-medium-Medium; do
  log "CEILING EVAL + z-tests: $ARM"
  "$PY" -m unciv_train.analyze_v5 --root "$ROOT/$ARM" ...

findings.title: bench-onnx grep filter silently drops failures — a timeout or crash produces no verdict line and the script continues
findings.description: The bench-onnx invocation pipes through `2>/dev/null | grep -E 'BENCH\||verdict=' || log "BENCH produced no verdict line"`. If the gradle subprocess times out (3600s), crashes, or produces output that doesn't match the grep pattern, the script logs a warning and continues — treating the gate as 'not failed' rather than 'failed'. A timeout is a gate failure (throughput is effectively zero), but the script doesn't exit non-zero or set a failure flag.
findings.rationale: A throughput gate that silently passes on timeout is worse than no gate at all — it gives false confidence that the model meets the ≥0.70 bar when it actually couldn't complete the benchmark. In a red-team review, any gate that can fail silently is a finding.
findings.suggested_addition: Capture the bench-onnx exit code separately from the grep. If the subprocess exits non-zero or the grep produces no match, treat it as a gate FAILURE (exit 1 or set a flag that the final log message reports). Do not let a timeout or crash be indistinguishable from a passing gate.
source_excerpts:
"$PY" -c "from unciv_train import run_loop as R; print(R.gradle_selfplay(['bench-onnx','$FINAL_ABS','200','Medium','$TH','777000'],3600.0))" \
      2>/dev/null | grep -E 'BENCH\||verdict=' || log "BENCH produced no verdict line"

findings.title: analyze_v5 writes acceptance_v5.json to the arm directory — a resumed run overwrites prior results
findings.description: `out_json = root / "acceptance_v5.json"` writes the ceiling eval result into the arm's run directory. If the experiment is re-run with `--resume` (e.g. after adding more rounds), analyze_v5 will overwrite the previous acceptance_v5.json. The prior result is lost — there's no timestamped or versioned output, so a researcher comparing two runs can't recover the earlier ceiling eval.
findings.rationale: Overwriting the sole results artifact on re-run is a data-loss footgun for a multi-hour experiment. A researcher who re-runs analyze_v5 to get updated numbers loses the original ceiling eval with no warning.
findings.suggested_addition: Before overwriting, check if acceptance_v5.json already exists and either (a) move it to acceptance_v5.json.bak, or (b) print a warning and require a `--force` flag to overwrite. At minimum, log that an existing file is being replaced.
source_excerpts:
out_json = root / "acceptance_v5.json"
    out_json.write_text(json.dumps(result, indent=2) + "\n")

findings.title: Diverged round in continual mode silently carries forward unchanged weights+opt, plateaus look like convergence
findings.description: On a non-finite loss the guard restores safe_state/safe_opt and returns with diverged=True. Under continual warm-start the safe_state at epoch 0 IS the prior round's final net — so a round that diverges on its very first epoch emits a checkpoint bit-identical to the previous round, and the next round warm-starts from it. The winrate curve then plateaus in a way indistinguishable from genuine convergence, corrupting the AC1/AC2 'beats 28.9%' conclusion.
findings.rationale: This is exactly FND-0028, marked DISMISSED in decisions.md on the grounds that 'export happens AFTER the round with the restored final net so the anchor stays consistent' — but that addresses on-policy anchor consistency, NOT the business question of whether a plateau is real learning or a stalled-but-still-emitting run. A researcher reading RESULTS.md cannot tell the difference.
findings.suggested_addition: run_v5.sh / analyze_v5 already read curve.csv which carries the `diverged` column — add a one-line guard in analyze_v5.main that counts diverged rounds and stamps `result['diverged_rounds']` + a WARN print before the ceiling-eval verdict, so a plateau driven by divergence is never silently read as convergence.
source_excerpts:
net.load_state_dict(safe_state)
                opt.load_state_dict(safe_opt)               # v5: restore optimizer moments too
                stats["note"] = f"non-finite loss at epoch {ep} — restored last-good weights"
                stats["diverged"] = True
                return net, stats

if args.continual:
            warm_net, warm_opt = net, opt                   # carry the persistent pair to next round

findings.title: Sidecar pruning deletes opt_round_{r-3}.pt but resume only needs r-1 — but mid-run crash window can orphan the carry
findings.description: Pruning runs gated on the SAME `--keep-shards`/shard-cleanup condition, deleting ckpt/opt for round r-3. But the curve.csv row (the resume cursor, written from CURVE_COLS) and the ckpt/opt sidecars are written at different points in the loop body. If the process dies after the curve row is appended for round r but before round r+1's ckpt lands, and a later --resume computes start_round from curve rows, _load_warm demands ckpt_round_{start-1}.pt — which is fine — but the comment claims 'keeping the last 3' while the code only unlinks exactly r-3 (not <r-3), so a non-contiguous run (resume gaps) leaves stale sidecars that never get pruned.
findings.rationale: FND-0013 (disk growth) is the stated motivation. The mismatch between the 'keep last 3' comment and the 'unlink exactly r-3' code means on any resumed/gappy run the prune misses rounds, so the disk-exhaustion risk the prune was added to fix is only partially mitigated for the exact long-run case it targets.
findings.suggested_addition: Add one assertion-style log line after pruning: `log stale-prune r-3 existed={...}` and either glob-prune all `*_round_{n}.pt` with n < r-2 or document that pruning is best-effort on contiguous runs. A single test asserting prune behavior across a simulated resume gap would catch it.

findings.title: _load_warm rebuilds the optimizer with args.lr, not the lr the carried Adam state was trained under
findings.description: On --resume, `opt = torch.optim.Adam(net.parameters(), lr=args.lr)` then loads the sidecar state_dict. The moment buffers (exp_avg/exp_avg_sq) are restored, but the lr/betas come from the current CLI args, not the persisted run config. If a resumed run is launched with a different --lr than the original (easy to do by hand when babysitting), the warm Adam moments are from a different learning regime, producing a run that is neither the uninterrupted run nor a clean fresh one — and nothing flags it.
findings.rationale: The whole continual-training claim (research-notes: 'resumed run == uninterrupted run, bit-for-bit') depends on the (net, opt, hyperparams) triple being consistent. The code validates net shape (load_state_dict raises) and sidecar presence, but not that lr/rung/variant match the original run — exactly the FND-0003 'fail-fast on incompatible checkpoint' intent, applied to weights only, not hyperparams.
findings.suggested_addition: Stamp the round's lr/variant/rung into a tiny `run_config.json` at round 0 and assert match in _load_warm (warn-and-continue at minimum). The test_warm_from_memory_equals_warm_from_disk test passes lr=1e-3 to both sides, so this divergence is uncovered by the suite today.
source_excerpts:
net.load_state_dict(torch.load(ckpt, weights_only=True))   # raises on shape/rung mismatch
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(net.parameters(), lr=args.lr)
    opt.load_state_dict(torch.load(optf, weights_only=True))

findings.title: analyze_v5 round-7 disentangling reads curve index by round value but uses dict.get(7) — off-by-one vs v4's 8 rounds
findings.description: The comment says 'round-8 (0-indexed) — matched to v4's 8 rounds' and reads wr_by_round.get(7). But the loop in run_loop writes curve rows with `round` = r where r ranges 0..rounds-1, so r=7 is the 8th completed round only if rounds are 0-indexed AND contiguous. v4 ran 8 rounds (r=0..7), so the 8th round is r=7 — correct IF v4 also 0-indexed. The disentangling claim ('v5 @ 8 rounds matched to v4') compares v5's r=7 winrate (an 80-game per-round eval) against v4's FINAL 200-game ceiling number (47/204) — different N and different eval protocol, so the regime-vs-round-count comparison is not apples-to-apples.
findings.rationale: FND-0029/R4 is the entire point of the round-7 field: to separate 'continual regime helped' from 'more rounds helped'. Comparing an 80-game per-round winrate to a 204-game ceiling winrate conflates eval-protocol noise with the regime signal — the conclusion drawn in RESULTS.md could be an artifact of the 80-vs-204 game-count mismatch.
findings.suggested_addition: Document in the result JSON that winrate_round7_80g is an 80-game per-round eval (not a ceiling eval) and is NOT directly z-testable against the 204-game v4 baseline; or run a dedicated 200-game eval on policy_round_7.onnx for a true matched comparison.
source_excerpts: result["winrate_round7_80g"] = wr_by_round.get(7)   # 8th round (0-indexed) — matched to v4's 8 rounds
V4_STRUCTURED = (47, 204)   # 23.0% — from-scratch small-rung structured (AC1 target to beat)

findings.title: micro-batch grad_norm stat is computed AFTER accumulation but compared to whole-batch at atol 1e-4 — fp summation-order may exceed it on Medium
findings.description: test_microbatch asserts grad_norm match within 1e-4 on a 6-step toy batch. The real Medium arm chunks ~1261-tile batches into K=256 sub-batches (5+ chunks), where float summation-order differences across many accumulation steps grow. The math is correct (size-weighted, per research-notes), but the 1e-4 tolerance was validated on 3 chunks, not on the production chunk count — a marginally-larger drift in the field won't fail any test but the gradient clipping (max_norm=10.0) could trip on one path and not the other, producing a genuinely different opt.step.
findings.rationale: The numerical-equivalence guarantee (AC6) is the load-bearing claim that lets ARM B's medium-rung result be treated as comparable to whole-batch training. The test validates equivalence at toy scale only; the clip-grad-norm interaction at production scale is untested.
findings.suggested_addition: Add a test variant with ~300 steps and K=256 (matching the real arm's chunk ratio) asserting grad_norm/weights still match within tolerance, OR log both pre-clip grad_norm values per round to metrics.jsonl so a divergence between the micro and whole paths is observable post-hoc.
source_excerpts:
gnorm = torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(net.parameters(), max_norm=10.0)
            opt.step()
assert abs(whole_stats["grad_norm"] - micro_stats["grad_norm"]) < 1e-4

findings.title: Missing circuit breaker for diverged runs wastes compute
findings.description: If a model diverges, `train_round` restores the safe state and sets `diverged=True` in the stats, but the round loop continues. The script will blindly execute the remaining rounds (generation, training, and evaluation) on a mathematically stuck model.
findings.rationale: Running 10+ hours of 12-core JVM compute on a model that has collapsed is pure waste. FinOps requires fail-fast mechanisms for unattended multi-hour jobs to prevent runaway cloud bills.
findings.suggested_addition: Add a circuit breaker after training: `if stats.get('diverged'): log('Diverged, aborting run'); break` to abort the arm and save compute.
source_excerpts: net, stats, mode, opt = train_round(

if args.continual:
            warm_net, warm_opt = net, opt

findings.title: Ceiling eval unconditionally runs on failed arms
findings.description: The script catches arm failures (`|| log 'ARM A FAILED'`) but the ceiling eval loop runs unconditionally for both arms. If an arm crashes at round 2, it will still run a full 200-game evaluation on `policy_round_2.onnx`.
findings.rationale: A 200-game JVM evaluation is computationally expensive. Running it on an incomplete or broken artifact wastes CPU hours and pollutes the final results.
findings.suggested_addition: Check for the expected final artifact before evaluating: `if [ ! -f "$ROOT/$ARM/policy_round_$((ROUNDS-1)).onnx" ]; then continue; fi`.
source_excerpts: for ARM in structured-small-Medium structured-medium-Medium; do
"$PY" -m unciv_train.analyze_v5 --root "$ROOT/$ARM" --label "$ARM" \
--ceiling-games 200

findings.title: Cost and throughput telemetry excluded from acceptance output
findings.description: The analysis script reports winrates and z-tests but ignores the `turns_per_sec` and `ms_per_decision` metrics that were carefully recorded in `metrics.jsonl`.
findings.rationale: Cost-per-unit tracking is essential for right-sizing. If the medium rung clears the baseline but costs 4x the CPU time per inference, that tradeoff must be visible in the final acceptance JSON to make informed architectural decisions.
findings.suggested_addition: Parse `metrics.jsonl` to extract the average `turns_per_sec` and include it in the `acceptance_v5.json` payload.
source_excerpts: result: dict = {"label": label, "root": str(root), "eval_seed": args.eval_seed,

out_json = root / "acceptance_v5.json"
    out_json.write_text(json.dumps(result, indent=2) + "\n")

findings.title: In-process state carryover lacks memory cleanup, risking late OOMs
findings.description: The loop carries `net` and `opt` in-process across 16 rounds. Without explicit garbage collection, memory fragmentation from the large trajectory tensors built during `train_round` can cause late-stage OOMs.
findings.rationale: An OOM at round 14 wastes all the compute from the previous 13 rounds. Explicit memory management is required to safely run long-lived stateful Python processes on right-sized instances without over-provisioning RAM.
findings.suggested_addition: Add `import gc` and call `gc.collect()` at the end of the round loop (around line 345) to ensure trajectory memory is fully reclaimed before the next generation phase.
source_excerpts: warm_net = warm_opt = None       # v5: the persistent continual (net, optimizer) pair
for r in range(start_round, args.rounds):
warm_net, warm_opt = net, opt                   # carry the persistent pair to next round
